locked_facts: First equation: v = u + at (velocity-time relation)
Second equation: s = ut + ½at² (displacement-time relation)
Third equation: v² = u² + 2as (velocity-displacement relation, eliminates time)
Displacement in nth second: s_n = u + a(n − ½) = u + a(2n−1)/2
All three equations are valid ONLY for constant (uniform) acceleration
For retardation (deceleration), acceleration a is negative in the direction of motion
Average velocity for uniform acceleration = (u + v)/2
truth_statements: The three equations of motion are derived from the definition of constant acceleration and are not independent — any one can be derived from the other two
These equations are valid only when acceleration is constant throughout the motion
The displacement in the nth second s_n = u + a(n − ½) is itself not constant — it changes each second by amount a
When a body decelerates to rest, v = 0 and the equations give stopping distance s = u²/(2|a|) and stopping time t = u/|a|
For free fall from rest, u = 0, a = g, giving v = gt, s = ½gt², v² = 2gs

aha_moment.what_to_show: A car braking to a stop. Three panels: (1) v-t graph showing linear decrease from u to 0, (2) s-t graph showing the parabola flattening, (3) a readout showing v² = u² + 2as predicting exact stopping distance. The predicted and actual stopping points match precisely.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: The third equation eliminates time — it directly connects velocity to displacement. The stopping distance predicted by v² = u² + 2as matches the simulation exactly.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students memorize formulas without seeing their predictive power. Watching the car stop at the exact predicted distance makes the equation feel real.

simulation_states.label: Setup
simulation_states.description: A ball on a frictionless track with initial velocity u and constant acceleration a. Velocity vector arrow shown. The three equations are displayed as formulas on the side panel.
simulation_states.emphasis: Initial conditions: u, a are given. The three equations will predict v, s, and t.

simulation_states.label: Equations in Action
simulation_states.description: Ball moves with constant acceleration. At each second, measured values of v, s are compared with predicted values from equations. A table fills up: t | v_measured | v_predicted | s_measured | s_predicted — all match.
simulation_states.emphasis: Every measured value matches the equation prediction — these equations are exact for constant acceleration.

simulation_states.label: Aha — Stopping Distance
simulation_states.description: A car with initial velocity u brakes with constant deceleration. Using v² = u² + 2as with v = 0: stopping distance s = u²/(2a) is calculated and a marker placed on the track. The car stops exactly at the marker.
simulation_states.emphasis: Third equation predicts stopping distance without needing time. Double the speed → four times the stopping distance (s ∝ u²).

simulation_states.label: Explore
simulation_states.description: Interactive: adjust initial velocity and acceleration. The ball moves and all three equation outputs update live. A toggle switches between acceleration and deceleration modes. s_n (displacement in nth second) is highlighted for each second.
simulation_states.emphasis: Double the initial speed and see stopping distance quadruple. Change acceleration and verify all three equations simultaneously.

common_misconceptions.belief: Equations of motion work for any kind of motion including non-uniform acceleration
trigger_phrases: har motion mein use kar sakte hain
common_misconceptions.correction: NCERT clearly states these equations are derived assuming CONSTANT acceleration. For variable acceleration, you must use calculus (v = ds/dt, a = dv/dt) and integrate.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show a case with changing acceleration where equation predictions fail vs simulation — mismatch demonstrates the constant-acceleration requirement

common_misconceptions.belief: Displacement in nth second is the total displacement after n seconds
trigger_phrases: s_n = ut + half at square with t = n
common_misconceptions.correction: s_n = u + a(n − ½) gives displacement during the nth second ONLY (from t = n−1 to t = n), not the total displacement. Total displacement after n seconds is s = un + ½an².
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show distance markers at each second: total displacement vs displacement-in-that-second highlighted separately with different colors

legend.meaning: Displacement in nth second = u + a(n−½)
